verses.commentary: Imitation of Paul is valid only as he imitates Christ. Discipleship chains establish patterns of Christlike living. Authority derives from conformity to Jesus, not personal charisma.
Paul commends their fidelity to apostolic tradition. Holding to teachings passed down reflects healthy submission to authoritative instruction.
Headship establishes ordered relationships: Christ over man, man over woman, God over Christ. This is relational structure, not value hierarchy. Order reflects Trinitarian pattern.
A man covering his head while praying or prophesying dishonors Christ, his head. Cultural symbols carry theological meaning. Proper worship requires culturally appropriate expressions of authority.
A woman uncovered dishonors her head (her husband). Shaved head signified shame or judgment. Cultural context shapes how honor and dishonor are communicated.
Logical consistency: if uncovered is acceptable, why not shaved? Since shaving is disgraceful, she should cover her head. Paul uses reductio ad absurdum.
Man reflects God's image and glory directly. Woman reflects man's glory, derived from him. Creation order informs worship practice, though both image God equally.
Priority in creation establishes relational pattern. Man wasn't derived from woman, but woman from man. Genesis 2 informs Paul's argument about worship order.
Purpose in creation: woman was made for man as helper and complement. This isn't inferiority but design, reflecting God's intended relational structure.
Authority on her head likely means the covering itself, a sign of proper order. "Because of the angels" may refer to their presence in worship or their role as guardians of created order.
Interdependence balances hierarchy. Neither sex exists independently in the Lord. Mutual need tempers structural order with relational equality.
Woman came from man in creation; man comes from woman through birth. All ultimately come from God. Reciprocity and divine origin relativize human distinctions.
Appeal to their own judgment. Does propriety not suggest a woman should be covered when praying? Cultural intuition confirms theological principle.
Nature itself teaches gender distinction through hair length. Men with long hair violate created norms. Natural order testifies to proper gender expression.
Long hair is a woman's glory and functions as a natural covering. God's design in creation provides the template for cultural expression in worship.
If anyone wants to argue, the churches have no other practice. Apostolic tradition and universal church practice settle disputes. Unity in practice reflects theological consensus.
Paul shifts from commendation to rebuke. Their gatherings do more harm than good, revealing deep dysfunction. Worship that damages is worse than no worship.
Divisions in the assembly contradict the unity the Lord's Supper signifies. Paul believes the reports, though not fully. Factions fracture the body.
Divisions are necessary to reveal who has God's approval. Testing exposes genuine faith. Conflict clarifies character and commitment.
When factions divide the meal, it's no longer the Lord's Supper. Unity is essential to the sacrament's meaning. Division nullifies communion.
Private suppers precede the communal meal, leaving some hungry while others get drunk. Economic disparity corrupts worship. The rich indulge while the poor are humiliated.
Eat at home if hungry. Despising the church and humiliating the poor perverts worship into injustice. Social inequality dishonors both God and community.
Paul received this tradition from the Lord and passed it to them. Apostolic transmission carries divine authority. The Last Supper's institution establishes sacramental pattern.
Jesus' broken body is given for them. The bread symbolizes His sacrificial death. "Do this in remembrance" makes the Supper a memorial reenactment.
The cup represents the new covenant in Christ's blood. Jeremiah's promise finds fulfillment in Jesus' death. Every drinking proclaims covenant renewal through sacrifice.
Eating and drinking proclaims the Lord's death until He returns. The Supper is eschatological, looking back to the cross and forward to the parousia. Memorial and anticipation converge.
Unworthy participation makes one guilty of sinning against Christ's body and blood. Casual or divisive eating profanes the sacrament. Self-examination is required.
Examine yourself before eating and drinking. Introspection precedes participation. The Supper demands sober self-assessment, not presumption.
Eating without discerning the body brings judgment. Failure to recognize the church as Christ's body or reverence the sacrament's significance incurs divine discipline.
Weakness, sickness, and death among them result from unworthy participation. God disciplines His people through physical consequences for sacramental profanation.
If we judged ourselves rightly, we wouldn't fall under judgment. Self-discipline through honest self-examination prevents divine discipline.
The Lord's discipline is corrective, not condemnatory. It preserves believers from final judgment with the world. Temporal suffering serves eternal salvation.
Wait for one another when gathering to eat. Mutual consideration and unity replace selfish consumption. Shared meals embody shared life in Christ.
If hungry, eat at home so gatherings don't result in judgment. Practical wisdom prevents sacramental abuse. Further instructions await Paul's visit.
